Association football league in Romania

This article is about the top division in men's Romanian football. **Liga I** (Romanian pronunciation: [[ˈliɡa ɨnˈtɨj]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Romanian "Help:IPA/Romanian"); *First League*), also spelled as **Liga 1** and officially known as **SuperLiga** for sponsorship reasons,[[1]](#cite_note-2) is a professional [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") league in [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania") and the highest level of the [Romanian football league system](/wiki/Romanian_football_league_system "Romanian football league system"). Contested by 16 clubs, it operates on a system of [promotion and relegation](/wiki/Promotion_and_relegation "Promotion and relegation") from and to [Liga II](/wiki/Liga_II "Liga II"). The teams play 30 matches each in the regular season, before entering the championship play-offs or the relegation play-outs according to their position in the regular table.

Liga I was established in 1909 and commenced play for the [1909–10 campaign](/wiki/1909%E2%80%9310_Divizia_A "1909–10 Divizia A"), being currently 25th in [UEFA](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA")'s [league coefficient](/wiki/UEFA_coefficient#League_coefficient "UEFA coefficient") ranking list. It is administered by the [Liga Profesionistă de Fotbal](/wiki/Liga_Profesionist%C4%83_de_Fotbal "Liga Profesionistă de Fotbal"), also known by the acronym LPF. Before the [2006–07 season](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_Liga_I "2006–07 Liga I"), the competition was known as *Divizia A*, but the name had to be changed following the finding that someone else had registered that trademark.[[2]](#cite_note-divizia-3)

The best performer to date is [FCSB](/wiki/CSA_Steaua_Bucure%C8%99ti_(football) "CSA Steaua București (football)") with 28 titles[[a]](#cite_note-Steaua-1) followed by [longtime cross-town](/wiki/Eternal_derby_(Romania) "Eternal derby (Romania)") rival [Dinamo București](/wiki/FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Dinamo București") with 18 trophies. Furthermore, of the remaining 21 clubs which came victorious in the competition, eight have won it on at least three occasions—[CFR Cluj](/wiki/CFR_Cluj "CFR Cluj") (eight trophies), [Venus București](/wiki/FC_Venus_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Venus București") (seven), [Chinezul Timișoara](/wiki/Chinezul_Timi%C8%99oara "Chinezul Timișoara") and [UTA Arad](/wiki/FC_UTA_Arad "FC UTA Arad") (six each), [Ripensia Timișoara](/wiki/FC_Ripensia_Timi%C8%99oara "FC Ripensia Timișoara"), [Universitatea Craiova](/wiki/FC_Universitatea_Craiova "FC Universitatea Craiova") and [Petrolul Ploiești](/wiki/FC_Petrolul_Ploie%C8%99ti "FC Petrolul Ploiești") (four each), and [Rapid București](/wiki/FC_Rapid_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Rapid București") (three).

## Competition format

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liga_I&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Competition format")]

Starting with 2020, the Liga I has been expanded to a 16-team format. After each team plays the others twice for 30 fixtures, they are [ranked](/wiki/Group_tournament_ranking_system "Group tournament ranking system") by total points and then divided according to their position to enter either the championship play-offs or the relegation play-outs. At this stage, the points are halved and criteria such as [goal difference](/wiki/Goal_difference "Goal difference"), goals scored etc. are erased completely.

The six clubs which enter the championship play-offs play ten games, while the remaining ten in the relegation play-outs will only play each other once, resulting in nine fixtures. The championship play-offs winners are also crowned winners of the season's Liga I. The 9th and 10th positions in the play-out are relegated directly to the [Liga II](/wiki/Liga_II "Liga II"), while the 7th- and 8th-placed teams will play a two-legged tie against the 3rd and 4th teams from the second league's table. From 2023/24, only 8th placed team will play a two-legged tie against 3rd placed team from second league's table.

Also, the 1st and 2nd teams from the play-out phase will play a one-legged game between each other and the winner will face the last team that completed the play-off phase in a European spot. The winner of that one-legged match will play next season in the [UEFA Europa Conference League](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_Conference_League "UEFA Europa Conference League").[[3]](#cite_note-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liga_I&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

### Early championships (1909–1921)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liga_I&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early championships (1909–1921)")]

[![group of 11 men in three rows, sitting, kneeling and standing, dressed in black and white football kit, flanked by 2 men in suits](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Olympia_FC_Bucuresti_in_1909.jpg/330px-Olympia_FC_Bucuresti_in_1909.jpg)](/wiki/File:Olympia_FC_Bucuresti_in_1909.jpg)

[Olympia București](/wiki/Olympia_Bucure%C8%99ti "Olympia București"), the [1909](/wiki/1909%E2%80%9310_Divizia_A "1909–10 Divizia A") champions.

The first official national football tournament was organized in [1909](/wiki/1909%E2%80%9310_Divizia_A "1909–10 Divizia A") by the recently founded [Romanian Football Federation](/wiki/Romanian_Football_Federation "Romanian Football Federation"), then called the *Association of Athletic Societies in Romania* ([Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language"): *Asociațiunea Societăților Atletice din România*). The final matches of the first [Romanian Football Championship](/wiki/1909%E2%80%9310_Divizia_A_(Cupa_ASAR) "1909–10 Divizia A (Cupa ASAR)") were held between December 1909 and January 1910 in [Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest").[[4]](#cite_note-lpf-5)[[5]](#cite_note-rsssf.com-6) The three pioneer clubs were [Olympia](/wiki/Olympia_Bucure%C8%99ti "Olympia București") and [Colentina](/wiki/Colentina_A.C._Bucure%C8%99ti "Colentina A.C. București") from Bucharest and [United](/wiki/Prahova_Ploie%C8%99ti "Prahova Ploiești") from [Ploiești](/wiki/Ploie%C8%99ti "Ploiești"). Each team played a fixture against the other two clubs, totalizing a number of three matches disputed, with [Olympia București](/wiki/Olympia_Bucure%C8%99ti "Olympia București") being crowned as champions of the first Romanian Football Championship.[[4]](#cite_note-lpf-5)[[6]](#cite_note-footrom-7) In the following years, the tournament was structured into regional groups with the winners of each group participating in a playoff with the eventual winners being declared champions. From 1909 until 1921, the championship was organized as a cup with the winner being crowned as Champions of Romania,[[4]](#cite_note-lpf-5)[[6]](#cite_note-footrom-7) except for between 1916 and 1919, when the competition was suspended due to [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I").[[7]](#cite_note-istoric-8) The champions of this period were [Olympia](/wiki/Olympia_Bucure%C8%99ti "Olympia București") and [Colentina](/wiki/Colentina_A.C._Bucure%C8%99ti "Colentina A.C. București"), each with two titles, and [United](/wiki/Prahova_Ploie%C8%99ti "Prahova Ploiești"), [Prahova](/wiki/Prahova_Ploie%C8%99ti "Prahova Ploiești"), [Venus](/wiki/AS_Venus_Bucure%C8%99ti "AS Venus București"), [Unirea Tricolor București](/wiki/Unirea_Tricolor_Bucure%C8%99ti "Unirea Tricolor București") and [Româno-Americana](/wiki/Rom%C3%A2no-American%C4%83_Bucure%C8%99ti "Româno-Americană București"), with one title each.[[4]](#cite_note-lpf-5)[[6]](#cite_note-footrom-7)

### Divizia A (1921–2006)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liga_I&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Divizia A (1921–2006)")]

The [1921–22 season](/wiki/1921%E2%80%9322_Divizia_A "1921–22 Divizia A") marked the first time when a league consisting of seven teams was formed. The championship, which had been confined to several regional leagues, became a national competition in 1921 with the foundation of Divizia A and [Divizia B](/wiki/Liga_II "Liga II"). The inaugural Divizia A season was won by [Chinezul Timișoara](/wiki/Chinezul_Timi%C8%99oara "Chinezul Timișoara").[[8]](#cite_note-beyond-9) Before the [1931–32 season](/wiki/1931%E2%80%9332_Divizia_A "1931–32 Divizia A"), the competition was dominated by Chinezul and [Venus București](/wiki/AS_Venus_Bucure%C8%99ti "AS Venus București"), with Chinezul winning six championships and Venus two championships during the eleven seasons.[[4]](#cite_note-lpf-5)[[8]](#cite_note-beyond-9) The [1932–33 season](/wiki/1932%E2%80%9333_Divizia_A "1932–33 Divizia A") saw the rise of another successful team, [Ripensia Timișoara](/wiki/FC_Ripensia_Timi%C8%99oara "FC Ripensia Timișoara"), which alongside rivals Venus, won eight of the following nine championships, before the competition was suspended in 1940 due to [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[4]](#cite_note-lpf-5)[[8]](#cite_note-beyond-9)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/UTA_Arad_1946-47.jpg/330px-UTA_Arad_1946-47.jpg)](/wiki/File:UTA_Arad_1946-47.jpg)

[UTA Arad](/wiki/FC_UTA_Arad "FC UTA Arad") during 1946–1947.

The post-war years were dominated by [UTA Arad](/wiki/FC_UTA_Arad "FC UTA Arad"), [CCA București](/wiki/FCSB "FCSB") and [Petrolul Ploiești](/wiki/FC_Petrolul_Ploie%C8%99ti "FC Petrolul Ploiești"). The 1960s saw the gradual emergence of [Dinamo București](/wiki/FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Dinamo București"), with the help from strikers [Gheorghe Ene](/wiki/Gheorghe_Ene "Gheorghe Ene") and [Florea Dumitrache](/wiki/Florea_Dumitrache "Florea Dumitrache")—both of whom became some of Divizia A's top all-time scorers. The 1970s saw the rise of [Dudu Georgescu](/wiki/Dudu_Georgescu "Dudu Georgescu"), from Dinamo București, who was Divizia A's leading scorer for four seasons between 1974 and 1978. He scored an impressive 156 goals and won the [European Golden Shoe](/wiki/European_Golden_Shoe "European Golden Shoe") award for the top scorer in Europe twice, in 1975 and 1977.[[9]](#cite_note-golden-10)[[10]](#cite_note-goalsc-11) Dinamo București also had two more European Golden Shoe winners in the 1986–87 season in the name of [Rodion Cămătaru](/wiki/Rodion_C%C4%83m%C4%83taru "Rodion Cămătaru") and in the 1988–89 season in the name of [Dorin Mateuţ](/wiki/Dorin_Mateu%C5%A3 "Dorin Mateuţ"), with the latter being the last Romanian winner of the trophy.[[9]](#cite_note-golden-10) From the [1959–60 season](/wiki/1959%E2%80%9360_Divizia_A "1959–60 Divizia A") all the way to the [1999–2000 season](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_Divizia_A "1999–2000 Divizia A") all the league championships were won by only seven teams: Steaua (16 titles), Dinamo (14 titles), [Universitatea Craiova](/wiki/FCU_Craiova "FCU Craiova") (4 titles), [Rapid București](/wiki/FC_Rapid_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Rapid București"), [FC Argeș](/wiki/FC_Arge%C8%99_Pite%C8%99ti "FC Argeș Pitești") and [UTA Arad](/wiki/FC_UTA_Arad "FC UTA Arad") (2 titles each), and [Petrolul Ploiești](/wiki/FC_Petrolul_Ploie%C8%99ti "FC Petrolul Ploiești") (one title).[[4]](#cite_note-lpf-5)

Dinamo București was the first Romanian team to qualify into the European Champions Cup in the [1956–57 season](/wiki/1956%E2%80%9357_European_Cup "1956–57 European Cup") of the competition and Universitatea Craiova was the last team from Romania to qualify in the [1991–92 season](/wiki/1991%E2%80%9392_European_Cup "1991–92 European Cup"), before the competition changed its name to the UEFA Champions League. Romanian teams qualified to 35 of the 37 seasons of the European Champions Cup, with Dinamo București having thirteen appearances, Steaua București having ten appearances, Universitatea Craiova having four appearances, Petrolul having three appearances, UTA Arad and FC Argeş having two appearances and Rapid București having one appearance. The most important results for a Romanian team in this competition were achieved by Steaua București which won the trophy in the [1985–86 season](/wiki/1985%E2%80%9386_European_Cup "1985–86 European Cup"), and reached the semi-finals in the [1987–88 season](/wiki/1987%E2%80%9388_European_Cup "1987–88 European Cup") and another final in the [1988–89 season](/wiki/1988%E2%80%9389_European_Cup "1988–89 European Cup").[[5]](#cite_note-rsssf.com-6) Other important achievements include Universitatea Craiova which reached the quarter-finals in the [1981–82 season](/wiki/1981%E2%80%9382_European_Cup "1981–82 European Cup") and Dinamo București which reached the semi-finals in the [1983–84 season](/wiki/1983%E2%80%9384_European_Cup "1983–84 European Cup").[[11]](#cite_note-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13) However, after the change of the format in [1992–93](/wiki/1992%E2%80%9393_UEFA_Champions_League "1992–93 UEFA Champions League") to the current Champions League format, Romanian champions have achieved limited successes, with Steaua only reaching the group stage three times before the 21st century.

The beginning of the 2000s were dominated by teams from the capital, with Steaua, Dinamo and Rapid winning all the league titles between 2000 and 2007.[[4]](#cite_note-lpf-5)

### Liga I (2006–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liga_I&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Liga I (2006–present)")]

At the beginning of the [2006–07 season](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_Liga_I "2006–07 Liga I") the competition was forced to change its name from Divizia A to **Liga I** due to a trademark dispute over the name.[[2]](#cite_note-divizia-3) The change was made on 15 May 2006, and the [Romanian Football Federation](/wiki/Romanian_Football_Federation "Romanian Football Federation") decided to also rename the lower leagues; thus Divizia B became [Liga II](/wiki/Liga_II "Liga II"), Divizia C became [Liga III](/wiki/Liga_III "Liga III"), and so on.[[2]](#cite_note-divizia-3) The [2006–07](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_Liga_I "2006–07 Liga I") season marked the 16th straight time a team from Bucharest won the championship, with Dinamo winning the title. Both [2007–08](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_Liga_I "2007–08 Liga I") and [2008–09](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_Liga_I "2008–09 Liga I") saw new title winners as [CFR Cluj](/wiki/CFR_Cluj "CFR Cluj") and [Unirea Urziceni](/wiki/FC_Unirea_Urziceni "FC Unirea Urziceni") were crowned champions for the first time.[[4]](#cite_note-lpf-5) CFR Cluj won their second championship in [2009–10](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_Liga_I "2009–10 Liga I"), while the [2010–11](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Liga_I "2010–11 Liga I") saw another new winner, [Oțelul Galați](/wiki/ASC_O%C8%9Belul_Gala%C8%9Bi "ASC Oțelul Galați"). Oțelul is the first and only club from the region of [Moldavia](/wiki/Western_Moldavia "Western Moldavia") to win a national title so far.

[![group of several men in several rows, standing, dressed in black and white football kit, flanked by man with camera](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Chelsea_%282%29_v_%281%29_CFR_Cluj.jpg/330px-Chelsea_%282%29_v_%281%29_CFR_Cluj.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chelsea_(2)_v_(1)_CFR_Cluj.jpg)

[CFR Cluj](/wiki/CFR_Cluj "CFR Cluj") (pictured at [Stamford Bridge](/wiki/Stamford_Bridge_(stadium) "Stamford Bridge (stadium)") in a [2008–09 UEFA Champions League](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_UEFA_Champions_League "2008–09 UEFA Champions League") match against [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.")) won eight championships under the new name of Liga I.

CFR Cluj, the 2007–08 winner became the first Romanian team to qualify directly into the [2008–09 group stage](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_UEFA_Champions_League "2008–09 UEFA Champions League") of the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League"), and the first team other than Steaua to qualify to this stage since the beginning of the new Champions League format in [1992–93](/wiki/1992%E2%80%9393_UEFA_Champions_League "1992–93 UEFA Champions League").[[13]](#cite_note-14) The [2009–10](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_Liga_I "2009–10 Liga I") champions as well as [2010–11](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Liga_I "2010–11 Liga I") ones were guaranteed a direct qualification spot into the group stage as well.[[14]](#cite_note-15) The best results in the group stage was obtained by CFR Cluj in [the 2012–13 UEFA Champions League](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_UEFA_Champions_League "2012–13 UEFA Champions League") with ten points and third place in a group with [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C."), [Braga](/wiki/S.C._Braga "S.C. Braga"), and [Galatasaray](/wiki/Galatasaray_S.K._(football) "Galatasaray S.K. (football)").

The 2010s also brought new league winners in Liga I, with [Astra Giurgiu](/wiki/FC_Astra_Giurgiu "FC Astra Giurgiu") and [Viitorul Constanța](/wiki/FC_Viitorul_Constan%C8%9Ba "FC Viitorul Constanța") clinching the titles in [2015–16](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_Liga_I "2015–16 Liga I") and [2016–17](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_Liga_I "2016–17 Liga I") respectively.[[15]](#cite_note-16) Since 2017 onwards, CFR Cluj won five consecutive Liga I titles, amassing a total number of eight national titles as of 2022. CFR Cluj obtained the best result of a Romanian team in the group stage in the [2019–2020](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_UEFA_Europa_League#Group_E "2019–20 UEFA Europa League") season of Europa League - 12 points. Also, CFR Cluj became the first Romanian team to qualify to UEFA Conference League group stage, when they obtained 4 points in the inaugural season (i.e. [2021–2022](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_UEFA_Europa_Conference_League#Group_D "2021–22 UEFA Europa Conference League")).[[16]](#cite_note-17)

In June 2022, Liga I officially changed its name into "SuperLiga" for sponsorship reasons, due to Romanian sporting bets agency Superbet sponsoring the competition.

## Sponsorship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liga_I&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Sponsorship")]

On 19 December 1998, [SABMiller](/wiki/SABMiller "SABMiller") bought the [naming rights](/wiki/Naming_rights "Naming rights") for four and a half seasons, becoming the first sponsor in the history of the competition. SABMiller changed the name of the competition to "Divizia A Ursus", to promote their [Ursus](/wiki/Ursus_Breweries "Ursus Breweries") beer.[[17]](#cite_note-18)

Starting with the 2004–05 season, [European Drinks & Foods](/wiki/European_Drinks_%26_Foods "European Drinks & Foods"), a Romanian $1.3 billion [USD](/wiki/US_Dollar "US Dollar") revenue company, took over as main sponsor and changed the league's name to "Divizia A Bürger", to promote their Bürger beer.[[18]](#cite_note-19)

On 11 May 2008, [Realitatea Media](/wiki/Realitatea-Ca%C8%9Bavencu "Realitatea-Cațavencu") bought the naming rights and changed the name of the competition to "Liga I Realitatea", to promote their [Realitatea TV](/wiki/Realitatea_TV "Realitatea TV") station.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

In late 2008, [European Drinks & Foods](/wiki/European_Drinks_%26_Foods "European Drinks & Foods") again bought the rights and the league was renamed as the "Liga I Frutti Fresh", after one of their soft drinks brand.[[20]](#cite_note-21)

For the 2009–10 season, the online betting firm [Gamebookers](/wiki/Bwin.Party_Digital_Entertainment "Bwin.Party Digital Entertainment") purchased the league naming rights and renamed the division "Liga 1 Gamebookers.com".[[21]](#cite_note-22)

In July 2010, Bergenbier, a StarBev Group company, bought the [naming rights](/wiki/Naming_rights "Naming rights") for four seasons and changed the name of the competition to "Liga I Bergenbier", to promote their Bergenbier beer.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

From the 2015–16 season, the French telecommunications corporation [Orange](/wiki/Orange_S.A. "Orange S.A.") became the main sponsor of the Romanian first league, after purchasing the league naming rights, for two years, and renamed the league in **Liga 1 Orange**.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

From the 2017–18 season, the international online gaming operator Betano became the main sponsor of the Romanian first league, after purchasing the league naming rights, for two years, and renamed the league in 'Liga 1 Betano'.

For the 2019–20 season, the national online gaming operator Casa Pariurilor became the main sponsor of the Romanian first league, after purchasing the league naming rights, and renamed the league in 'Casa Liga 1'.

For the 2022–23 season the operator of games in Romania Superbet is the sponsor of the leagues and the name changes to Superliga României.

## Media coverage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liga_I&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Media coverage")]

In 2004, [Telesport](/wiki/Telesport "Telesport"), a small TV network, bought the broadcasting rights for [$](/wiki/USD "USD")28 million. The four seasons contract ended in the summer of 2008. Telesport sold some of the broadcasting rights for matches to other Romanian networks, including, [TVR1](/wiki/TVR1 "TVR1"), [Antena 1](/wiki/Antena_1_(Romania) "Antena 1 (Romania)"), [Național TV](/w/index.php?title=Na%C8%9Bional_TV&action=edit&redlink=1 "Național TV (page does not exist)"), and [Kanal D](/wiki/Kanal_D "Kanal D").

On 31 March 2008, Antena 1 with [RCS & RDS](/wiki/RCS_%26_RDS "RCS & RDS") outbid [Realitatea Media](/wiki/Realitatea-Ca%C8%9Bavencu "Realitatea-Cațavencu") and [Kanal D](/wiki/Kanal_D "Kanal D") in the broadcasting rights auction with a bid of €102 million for a three seasons contract.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

In 2011, the broadcasting rights were bought by [RCS & RDS](/wiki/RCS_%26_RDS "RCS & RDS") for their channels [Digi Sport 1](/wiki/Digi_Sport_1 "Digi Sport 1"), [Digi Sport 2](/wiki/Digi_Sport_2 "Digi Sport 2") and [Digi Sport 3](/wiki/Digi_Sport_3 "Digi Sport 3"). This channels aired broadcasting of seven of the nine matches from each stage of the championship. The other two matches were broadcast by [Antena 1](/wiki/Antena_1_(Romania) "Antena 1 (Romania)") (an [Intact Media Group](/wiki/Intact_Media_Group "Intact Media Group") channel) and [Dolce Sport](/wiki/Dolce_Sport "Dolce Sport") (a channel owned by [Telekom Romania](/wiki/Telekom_Romania "Telekom Romania")).

In March 2014, LPF announced that the rights were sold for a five-year period to a company from the European Union, without specifying the company's name.[[25]](#cite_note-26) A month later, [Look TV](/wiki/Look_TV "Look TV") and Look Plus were revealed as the TV stations that would broadcast the games from Liga I and [Cupa Ligii](/wiki/Cupa_Ligii "Cupa Ligii") between 2014 and 2019.[[26]](#cite_note-27)

## EA Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Liga_I&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: EA Sports")]

On 27 August 2019, Liga I signed a contract with [EA Sports](/wiki/EA_Sports "EA Sports") for the rights of the league for [FIFA 20](/wiki/FIFA_20 "FIFA 20"). It was the first time that the Liga I had been featured in a sports video game.[[27]](#cite_note-28) Liga I has been featured in every FIFA installment since then.
